| `csv.DictReader` | Reads the CSV and maps each row to an `OrderedDict` using the **first row as field names**. |
| `json.dumps(row)` | Serialises each row-dict to a compact JSON string. |
| `+ "\n"` | Appends a newline so every JSON object occupies exactly **one line** (JSON Lines format). |
| Output file | Each line in `/tmp/data.jsonl` is a self-contained, valid JSON object. |
| `csv.DictReader` | Reads the CSV and automatically uses the **first row as the header**, turning every subsequent row into an `OrderedDict` keyed by column name. |
| `json.dumps(row)` | Serialises each row dictionary to a compact JSON string. |
| `+ "\n"` | Appends a newline after each object, producing valid **JSON Lines** format (one JSON object per line). |

If you don't have `jq` installed, you can also use Python:
find . -name "*.json" -exec sh -c 'python3 -m json.tool "$1" > temp && mv temp "$1"' _ {} \;
find . -name "*.json" | while read f; do echo "=== $f ===" && jq . "$f"; done
for f in $(find . -name "*.json"); do echo "=== $f ==="; jq . "$f"; done
widths = [max(len(str(row[i])) for row in rows) for i in range(len(rows[0]))]
print('  '.join(f"{col:<{width}}" for col, width in zip(row, widths)))
col_widths = [max(len(row[i]) for row in rows) for i in range(len(rows[0]))]
print('  '.join(f"{cell:<{col_widths[i]}}" for i, cell in enumerate(row)))
Or with a command line tool:
**Pretty-print the entire response first** (to inspect structure):
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://example.com | grep -q "^200$" && echo YES || echo NO
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}\n" https://example.com | grep -q "^200$" && echo YES || echo NO
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://example.com | grep -q "^200$" && echo YES || echo NO
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://example.com | grep -q "^200$" && echo YES || echo NO
curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}\n" https://example.com | sd '^200$' YES | sd '^(?!YES).*' NO
fd -e tar.gz /tmp | while read file; do echo "$(sha256sum "$file" | cut -d' ' -f1)  $file"; done
